task: I’m trying to explain to a friend why a part can look fine at first but still cause trouble later in the process, and I want to keep it simple and accurate. Can you help me phrase that in plain English without making it sound too technical?

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here are a few plain-English ways to say it:

- **“A part can look okay at first, but still have a small issue that only shows up later when it’s used or checked in the next step.”**
- **“It may pass a quick look, but still cause problems later if something was missed early on.”**
- **“Sometimes a part looks fine on the surface, but a hidden problem doesn’t show up until the next process.”**

If you want, I can make it sound:
- more casual,
- more professional,
- or even shorter for a quick conversation.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. I’d want it a little tighter and more plain. Something like:

“Yeah, it can look fine at first and still have a small problem that shows up later when it gets used or checked again.”

Or even simpler:

“Looks good now doesn’t always mean it’ll hold up later.”

That’s the kind of thing I’d say.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s a good way to say it.

A few tight, plain-English options:

- **“It can look fine at first and still have a small problem that shows up later.”**
- **“Looking good now doesn’t always mean it’ll hold up later.”**
- **“A part can pass a quick look and still cause trouble down the line.”**

If you want, I can make it sound a little more like:
- something you’d say to a friend,
- something you’d say at work,
- or something even shorter and punchier.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s it. Plain and tight. I’d probably use the second one:

“Looking good now doesn’t always mean it’ll hold up later.”

That lands right.
